If injecting the anterior scalene with lidocaine temporarily relieves symptoms, decompression will work in ~90% of cases. If the block doesn't help, don't operate — you have the wrong diagnosis. The block is your 'test drive' of the surgery.

name: Mesenteric Bypass / SMA Stenting
overview: Revascularization for chronic mesenteric ischemia

pimpingQuestions.question: The celiac trunk, SMA, and IMA develop from ventral segmental arteries of the aorta. Why do patients with chronic mesenteric ischemia typically need TWO of these three vessels to be diseased before developing symptoms?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Because the three mesenteric arteries are connected by a robust embryologically-derived collateral network: the pancreaticoduodenal arcades (celiac ↔ SMA), the arc of Riolan / marginal artery of Drummond (SMA ↔ IMA), and the arc of Bühler (embryonic ventral anastomosis between celiac and SMA, persists in 2-4%). These collaterals can compensate for single-vessel occlusion. Symptoms (postprandial pain, food fear, weight loss) only develop when two of three vessels are critically stenosed/occluded and the remaining collateral network is overwhelmed.

pimpingQuestions.question: You're performing an antegrade aortomesenteric bypass from the supraceliac aorta to the SMA. How do you expose the supraceliac aorta, and what structure do you divide to get there?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Perform a left medial visceral rotation (Mattox maneuver) or approach through the lesser sac by dividing the gastrohepatic ligament. Then divide the median arcuate ligament (the muscular crus of the diaphragm) — this crosses over the aorta at the aortic hiatus and often compresses the celiac trunk (median arcuate ligament syndrome). Dividing it exposes the supraceliac aorta for clamping and anastomosis. The supraceliac aorta is typically FREE of atherosclerotic disease (unlike the infrarenal), which is why it's preferred for inflow.

pimpingQuestions.question: After SMA stenting, you're monitoring for restenosis. What's the duplex ultrasound velocity threshold that indicates >70% SMA stenosis, and why is SMA stent surveillance different from other arterial stents?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Peak systolic velocity >275 cm/s in the SMA indicates >70% stenosis (some labs use >300 cm/s). SMA stent surveillance is different because: (1) the SMA has high-resistance flow at rest and LOW-resistance flow postprandially — you must do fasting AND postprandial studies, (2) in-stent restenosis rates are higher for mesenteric stents (20-40% at 3 years) than other vascular beds due to the aortic origin angle and respiratory-related stent stress, (3) restenosis can be clinically silent until acute thrombosis causes mesenteric infarction — a catastrophic event. Surveillance duplex every 6 months is mandatory.

pimpingQuestions.question: You've completed a retrograde SMA bypass from the infrarenal aorta. The patient does well for 2 days then develops acute abdominal pain, lactic acidosis, and shock. What happened?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Graft thrombosis causing acute mesenteric ischemia — the most feared complication. Retrograde grafts (originating from infrarenal aorta) have a higher kink/thrombosis rate than antegrade grafts (from supraceliac aorta) because the graft must make a sharp curve from the infrarenal aorta up to the SMA. This is an emergency: immediate return to OR for graft thrombectomy and revision, assessment of bowel viability, resection of necrotic bowel. Mortality of acute graft thrombosis is 50-80%. This is why many vascular surgeons prefer antegrade bypass despite the more demanding exposure.

pimpingQuestions.question: Endovascular SMA stenting vs open bypass — when do you choose each, and what's the honest long-term data?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Stenting first for most patients: lower morbidity (5% vs 15% for open), shorter hospital stay, and good initial results. BUT: primary patency at 3 years is only 50-65% for stents vs 85-90% for open bypass. The recurrence rate for symptoms is significantly higher after stenting. Choose OPEN bypass when: (1) young, good-risk patient who needs durable repair, (2) failed prior stenting, (3) anatomy unfavorable for stenting (flush occlusion, heavy calcification at the origin), (4) concurrent aortic pathology requiring open repair. The real pearl: ALWAYS stent or bypass BOTH celiac AND SMA when doing open repair — the recurrence rate after single-vessel revascularization is unacceptable because the other vessel will progress.

name: Tunneled Dialysis Catheter Placement
overview: Placement of long-term hemodialysis access catheter

pimpingQuestions.question: The right internal jugular vein develops from the anterior cardinal vein. Why is the RIGHT IJ the preferred access site for tunneled dialysis catheters over the LEFT?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The right anterior cardinal vein provides a STRAIGHT path to the SVC and right atrium — the right IJ runs directly into the right brachiocephalic vein, then straight into the SVC. The left IJ must cross the midline via the left brachiocephalic vein, traversing at an angle over the aortic arch. This creates: (1) more catheter kinking at the left brachiocephalic vein, (2) higher risk of central venous stenosis from catheter impingement on the vein wall, (3) worse flow rates due to the curved trajectory. Left IJ catheters have higher malfunction rates and shorter catheter life.

pimpingQuestions.question: You're using ultrasound to access the right IJ. What structure lies immediately posterior to the IJ in the carotid sheath, and what landmark tells you you're too low in the neck?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The vagus nerve lies between and posterior to the IJ and carotid artery within the carotid sheath. Needle injury causes hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal branch) or hemodynamic instability (carotid body stimulation). You're too low when you see the confluence of the IJ and subclavian vein (the venous angle) — access here risks pneumothorax and makes it difficult to create the subcutaneous tunnel. Ideal access is at the apex of the triangle formed by the two heads of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, typically at the level of the cricoid cartilage.

pimpingQuestions.question: The catheter tip should rest at the cavoatrial junction. What happens if the tip is too high (mid-SVC), and what happens if it's too low (right atrium)?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Too high (mid-SVC): catheter abuts the SVC wall, causing fibrin sheath formation, vessel wall erosion, and eventually SVC stenosis/thrombosis. Flow rates are also lower because the lumens suck against the wall (especially during recirculation positioning). Too low (deep in RA or across tricuspid valve): risk of cardiac arrhythmias (catheter irritating the conduction system), tricuspid valve damage, cardiac perforation with tamponade, and right atrial thrombus. The sweet spot is the cavoatrial junction — confirmed on fluoroscopy as the catheter tip at the junction of the SVC shadow and the right heart border.

pimpingQuestions.question: Your dialysis patient's catheter flows well for 3 months, then develops progressively poor flow with high venous pressures during dialysis. Lines have been reversed, TPA locked, and it still doesn't work. What's the diagnosis?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Fibrin sheath formation — a biofilm sleeve that encases the entire catheter from insertion site to tip, acting as a one-way valve that allows infusion but impairs aspiration. It's the #1 cause of late catheter dysfunction, occurring in virtually 100% of catheters by 3 months. Treatment options: (1) fibrin sheath stripping — snare the catheter tip from a femoral approach and strip the sheath, (2) catheter exchange over a wire with balloon disruption of the sheath, (3) TPA infusion (already failed in this case). Simply exchanging the catheter without disrupting the sheath leads to recurrence because the sheath remains in the vein.

pimpingQuestions.question: A patient develops massive lower extremity lymphedema after bilateral inguinofemoral lymph node dissection. What's the rate, and what could you have done differently?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Rate: 30-70% after bilateral inguinofemoral lymph node dissection — it's the most disabling long-term complication. Prevention strategies: (1) Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) — the GROINSS-V and GOG-173 trials validated SLNB for vulvar cancer <4cm with clinically negative groins, reducing lymphedema to <5%, (2) preserve the saphenous vein (Catalona modification) — reduces lymphedema by 30-50%, (3) sartorius muscle transposition over the femoral vessels (protects vessels and reduces dead space), (4) meticulous groin wound care with closed suction drains for 7-14 days. The paradigm shift: we no longer do radical en bloc vulvectomy with bilateral groin dissection for every vulvar cancer. SLNB has revolutionized vulvar cancer surgery just as it did for melanoma and breast cancer. If the sentinel node is negative, skip the full dissection.

name: Robotic Ventral Hernia Repair (rTAR)
overview: Robot-assisted transversus abdominis release for ventral hernia

pimpingQuestions.question: The transversus abdominis muscle develops from which embryologic myotome, and what is the transversus abdominis release (TAR)?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The lateral plate mesoderm (body wall musculature). TAR involves dividing the transversus abdominis muscle lateral to the rectus sheath, entering the preperitoneal space (space of Retzius anteriorly, Bogros space laterally). This releases the posterior rectus sheath medially, allowing tension-free midline fascial closure and wide mesh overlap in the retromuscular (sublay) position.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the anatomy of the retromuscular (Rives-Stoppa) space?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The space between the rectus abdominis muscle (anterior) and the posterior rectus sheath (posterior). Above the arcuate line, the posterior sheath is formed by the aponeuroses of the internal oblique and transversus abdominis. Below the arcuate line, there IS no posterior sheath — all aponeuroses pass anterior, leaving only transversalis fascia and peritoneum posteriorly.

pimpingQuestions.question: What nerves are at risk during TAR, and what are the consequences of injury?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Intercostal nerves (T7-T12) and the subcostal nerve (T12) — they run between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis (where the TAR division occurs). Injury causes abdominal wall denervation: bulging (pseudo-hernia) without a fascial defect. The nerves must be identified and preserved during lateral dissection of the transversus abdominis.

pimpingQuestions.question: What type of mesh and position is used in robotic TAR, and why is this position biomechanically superior?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Wide-pore polypropylene mesh in the retromuscular (sublay) position. This is superior because: 1) Pascal's law — intra-abdominal pressure pushes the mesh against the abdominal wall (self-reinforcing), 2) The mesh is between two vascularized tissue layers promoting incorporation, 3) No intraperitoneal mesh (avoids adhesions and bowel complications). Mesh overlap: ≥5cm beyond the fascial defect in all directions.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the recurrence rate of rTAR compared to other ventral hernia repair techniques?
pimpingQuestions.answer: rTAR recurrence: 2-5% at 2-3 years (early data, promising). Open Rives-Stoppa: 5-10%. Laparoscopic IPOM (intraperitoneal onlay mesh): 10-15%. Open onlay: 15-25%. Primary suture repair: 30-50%. The retromuscular position with TAR component separation has the lowest recurrence rate for complex ventral hernias, and the robotic approach reduces wound complications compared to open.

name: Robotic eTEP Inguinal Hernia Repair
overview: Robot-assisted extended totally extraperitoneal inguinal hernia repair

pimpingQuestions.question: The inguinal canal develops from the processus vaginalis. What is it, and what pathology results from its failure to close?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The processus vaginalis is a peritoneal outpouching that follows the testis through the inguinal canal during descent (weeks 28-32). Failure to close: patent processus vaginalis → indirect inguinal hernia (peritoneal sac through the internal ring along the spermatic cord). Complete patency: communicating hydrocele. Partial: encysted hydrocele of the cord.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the eTEP (enhanced totally extraperitoneal) approach, and how does it differ from standard TEP?
pimpingQuestions.answer: eTEP uses a crossover technique: ports are placed on the contralateral side, and a retromuscular (retrorectus) plane is developed crossing the midline to reach the affected groin. This provides a larger working space than standard TEP (which enters the preperitoneal space directly), allowing better visualization and mesh deployment. It is also ideal for bilateral inguinal hernias in one setting.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the 'triangle of doom' and 'triangle of pain' in the myopectineal orifice?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Triangle of doom: bounded by the vas deferens (medially) and gonadal vessels (laterally) — contains the external iliac artery and vein. Tacking/stapling here causes vascular injury. Triangle of pain: lateral to the gonadal vessels, bounded by the iliopubic tract — contains the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, femoral branch of genitofemoral nerve, and femoral nerve. Tacking here causes chronic neuralgia.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply to the spermatic cord structures that must be preserved?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Three arteries: 1) Testicular artery (from aorta — in the cord), 2) Artery of the vas (from inferior vesical artery), 3) Cremasteric artery (from inferior epigastric). The pampiniform venous plexus provides venous drainage. During hernia repair, at least one arterial supply must be preserved or the testis may atrophy (ischemic orchitis: 0.5-1% after inguinal hernia repair).

pimpingQuestions.question: What mesh size is used in eTEP inguinal repair, and what is the critical overlap over the myopectineal orifice?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Medium-weight polypropylene, minimum 10 x 15 cm (often 12 x 17 cm). Must cover the entire myopectineal orifice with ≥5cm overlap over the direct, indirect, and femoral spaces. The mesh is placed in the preperitoneal position and held by intra-abdominal pressure (no fixation needed in most cases — reducing chronic pain). The mesh must extend medially past the pubic symphysis.

name: Open Shouldice Repair
overview: Tension-free tissue-only repair of inguinal hernia
indications: Primary inguinal hernia in young patients

pimpingQuestions.question: The transversalis fascia is the key structure repaired in the Shouldice technique. What embryologic layer does it represent?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The transversalis fascia is the deep investing fascia of the transversus abdominis muscle — derived from the somatic mesoderm body wall. It forms the posterior wall of the inguinal canal. In direct hernias, the transversalis fascia has attenuated/failed in the Hesselbach triangle; the Shouldice repair reconstructs this layer with a multi-layer imbrication.

pimpingQuestions.question: What are the four layers of the Shouldice repair?
pimpingQuestions.answer: After opening the transversalis fascia and exploring the preperitoneal space: Layer 1: medial flap of transversalis fascia sutured to the iliopubic tract (shelving edge of inguinal ligament). Layer 2: return stitch — lateral flap to conjoint tendon. Layer 3: internal oblique/conjoined tendon to inguinal ligament. Layer 4: return stitch. All with continuous non-absorbable monofilament (stainless steel wire at the original Shouldice Hospital).

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply to the inguinal canal floor, and why does the Shouldice repair work?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The transversalis fascia receives blood supply from the deep circumflex iliac artery, inferior epigastric artery, and cremasteric artery branches. The Shouldice works by imbrication (overlapping repair) of these fascial layers, creating a multi-layered reinforcement of the posterior wall. The continuous running suture distributes tension evenly. At the Shouldice Hospital, the recurrence rate is 0.5-1% (compared to 2-4% for Lichtenstein mesh repair in some series).

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the main advantage and disadvantage of the Shouldice compared to mesh repair?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Advantage: no mesh (no foreign body complications — chronic pain from mesh, mesh infection, mesh migration, orchitis). The Shouldice Hospital has lower chronic pain rates (1-2%) than mesh repair (5-10%). Disadvantage: the technique is difficult to reproduce outside the Shouldice Hospital — in general surgical practice, recurrence rates with Shouldice are higher (4-10%) versus Lichtenstein (1-2%).

pimpingQuestions.question: For which patient populations is a pure tissue repair (Shouldice) preferred over mesh?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Young active patients (<30), women (low recurrence risk, mesh complications disproportionately affect women), contaminated/infected fields (mesh contraindicated), strangulated hernias with bowel resection, and patients who refuse mesh. The European Hernia Society (EHS) guidelines recommend mesh for most adult male inguinal hernias but acknowledge tissue repair for selected patients.

name: Sports Hernia Repair (Athletic Pubalgia)
overview: Repair of chronic groin pain in athletes without obvious hernia

pimpingQuestions.question: What is a 'sports hernia' — does it involve an actual fascial defect?
pimpingQuestions.answer: No — athletic pubalgia is NOT a true hernia. It's a chronic musculotendinous injury involving the pubic symphysis region: rectus abdominis insertion, adductor longus origin, or the conjoint tendon attachment on the pubic tubercle. There is no fascial defect or peritoneal sac. The term 'sports hernia' is a misnomer that persists in common usage.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the anatomy of the pubic symphysis region relevant to athletic pubalgia?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The pubic symphysis is the central anchor point where opposing force vectors meet: the rectus abdominis/obliques pull superiorly, and the adductor longus/brevis pull inferiorly. The conjoint tendon, inguinal ligament, and transversalis fascia all attach here. Chronic shearing stress at these attachments causes micro-tears and chronic pain. The 'pubic plate' concept describes this shared tendinous insertion.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply to the pubic region, and why does it heal poorly?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The pubic symphysis and tendinous attachments are supplied by branches of the obturator artery, inferior epigastric artery, and medial femoral circumflex artery. The tendon-bone junction (enthesis) has relatively poor vascularity — similar to rotator cuff tendinopathy, this watershed zone heals slowly and is prone to chronic degeneration.

pimpingQuestions.question: What surgical approach is used for athletic pubalgia repair?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Options: 1) Minimal repair (Muschaweck technique): reinforcement of the posterior inguinal wall with mesh and reinforcement of the conjoint tendon. 2) Laparoscopic/robotic preperitoneal mesh placement (TEP/TAPP). 3) Adductor tenotomy (if adductor longus is the primary pathology). Often combined: inguinal floor reinforcement + adductor longus release. Return to sport: 6-12 weeks.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the non-operative success rate, and when should you operate?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Conservative therapy (rest, physical therapy focusing on core/hip stabilization, anti-inflammatories) succeeds in 50-60% at 3-6 months. Surgery is indicated when 3-6 months of conservative management fails in a patient who needs to return to high-level athletics. Surgical success rate: 85-95% return to sport. MRI is important to rule out: osteitis pubis, femoral acetabular impingement, labral tear, and true inguinal hernia.

name: Parastomal Hernia Repair
overview: Repair of hernia around ostomy site

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the incidence of parastomal hernia after end colostomy creation, and what anatomic factor is most predictive?
pimpingQuestions.answer: 30-50% at 5 years (the most common stoma complication). Most predictive factor: stoma placement lateral to the rectus muscle (2-3x higher risk than through the rectus). Other factors: obesity, COPD, steroids, malnutrition, and large fascial defect at stoma creation.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the anatomy of a parastomal hernia — what herniates and through what defect?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Abdominal contents (omentum, small bowel, colon) herniate through the fascial defect around the stoma, alongside the bowel. The defect enlarges over time as the fascia stretches around the stoma conduit. The hernia sac follows the path of least resistance — usually lateral to the stoma where the lateral fascial support is weakest.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply concern when repairing a parastomal hernia?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The stoma's mesentery and its marginal artery must be preserved during repair — devascularization causes stoma necrosis or ischemia. During mesh placement, the mesh aperture around the stoma must be sized correctly: too tight compresses the mesentery (ischemia), too loose allows hernia recurrence.

pimpingQuestions.question: What are the surgical options for parastomal hernia repair?
pimpingQuestions.answer: 1) Local fascial repair (primary suture — recurrence 50-70%, not recommended). 2) Stoma relocation (moving to a new site — recurrence 30% at the new site). 3) Mesh repair: Sugarbaker (intraperitoneal mesh, bowel lateralized under mesh flap), Keyhole (mesh with aperture for bowel — high recurrence at keyhole), or modified Sugarbaker-Keyhole hybrid. 4) Retromuscular mesh (open or robotic — lowest recurrence 5-15%).

pimpingQuestions.question: What does the PREVENT trial show about prophylactic mesh placement at the time of index stoma creation?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The PREVENT trial (and STOMAMESH trial): prophylactic mesh (placed in the retromuscular or preperitoneal position) at the time of end colostomy creation reduces parastomal hernia rates from 40-50% to 10-15% at 2 years. The mesh does not increase surgical site infection or stoma complications. Prophylactic mesh is now recommended by the European Hernia Society for permanent end colostomies.

name: Open Hiatal Hernia Repair
overview: Open surgical repair of large hiatal hernia

pimpingQuestions.question: The esophageal hiatus is formed by the right crus of the diaphragm in most people. What percentage have contributions from the left crus?
pimpingQuestions.answer: 15-20% have contributions from the left crus or have bilateral crural involvement. The right crus alone forms the hiatus in 80-85%. This is clinically relevant because the crural anatomy determines the repair strategy — asymmetric or weak crura require more extensive reconstruction.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the anatomy of the phrenoesophageal ligament, and how does its failure contribute to hiatal hernia?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The phrenoesophageal ligament (Laimer's membrane) is a fibroelastic membrane connecting the esophagus circumferentially to the diaphragmatic hiatus. It anchors the GEJ at the hiatus. Age-related degeneration, increased intra-abdominal pressure, and connective tissue weakness cause this ligament to attenuate, allowing the GEJ (Type I) or stomach (Type II-IV) to herniate.

pimpingQuestions.question: During open hiatal hernia repair, what is the blood supply to the vagus nerves, and why is their preservation important?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The vagus nerves receive blood supply from the vasa nervorum (small arteries accompanying the nerve trunk from the esophageal arterial plexus). Injury to the vagus causes gastroparesis, dumping syndrome, and diarrhea. The anterior vagus (left, often a single trunk at the hiatus) is most at risk during anterior dissection. The posterior vagus runs on the posterior esophageal surface.

pimpingQuestions.question: When is open repair preferred over laparoscopic for hiatal hernias?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Open approach preferred when: giant paraesophageal hernia with significant mediastinal adhesions/incarceration, concurrent procedure requiring laparotomy, hostile abdomen from prior surgery, and surgeon preference/experience. However, most evidence shows laparoscopic repair has equivalent outcomes with less morbidity — open approach is increasingly reserved for the most complex cases.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the short esophagus, and how do you manage it during hiatal hernia repair?
pimpingQuestions.answer: A foreshortened esophagus (from chronic inflammation/fibrosis, Barrett's, or previous surgery) that cannot be reduced to ≥3cm of intra-abdominal length without tension. True short esophagus occurs in 5-15% of giant hiatal hernias. Management: Collis gastroplasty — a longitudinal staple line along the lesser curve creates a neo-esophagus from the gastric cardia, adding 3-5cm of length. The fundoplication is then wrapped around the neo-esophagus.

name: Robotic Hiatal Hernia Repair
overview: Robot-assisted repair of hiatal hernia with fundoplication

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the embryologic origin of the diaphragmatic crura?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The crura develop from the dorsal mesentery of the esophagus (mesoesophagus) — not from the septum transversum or pleuroperitoneal membranes. This unique origin explains why the crura are striated muscle (unlike the diaphragmatic dome which is mixed) and are innervated by the phrenic nerve separately from the costal diaphragm.

pimpingQuestions.question: What specific advantage does the robotic platform offer for hiatal hernia repair?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Wristed instruments allow precise crural suturing in the deep, narrow space behind the esophagus (posterior cruroplasty). The 3D magnification enables identification and preservation of the vagus nerves and visualization of the short gastric vessels. The stable camera platform eliminates assistant-dependent visualization issues. These advantages are most evident in large paraesophageal hernia repairs.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply to the crural muscle used for repair?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The crura receive blood supply from the inferior phrenic arteries (from aorta) and musculophrenic arteries. The crural muscle is generally well-vascularized, supporting suture-based repair. However, attenuated crura in large chronic hernias may have poor tissue quality, necessitating mesh reinforcement.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the recurrence rate after robotic hiatal hernia repair, and how does mesh affect it?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Recurrence: 5-15% (radiographic) at 5 years, comparable to laparoscopic rates. Biologic mesh reinforcement over the crural repair may reduce recurrence (some studies show reduction from 22% to 9%), but evidence is conflicting. Synthetic mesh is controversial due to erosion risk into the esophagus. The crural repair itself (posterior cruroplasty with pledgets) is the most important factor.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the 'short gastric vessels' assessment during robotic hiatal hernia repair?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Division of the short gastric vessels (from the splenic artery, 2-6 vessels) is performed to fully mobilize the gastric fundus for a tension-free fundoplication wrap. Robotically, this is facilitated by the articulating energy device. Failure to divide the short gastrics creates a tight wrap → dysphagia. The fundus must reach the right crus without tension (the 'shoe-shine' test: the fundus should easily pass behind the esophagus and return).

name: Wide Local Excision (Melanoma)
overview: Wide surgical excision of melanoma with appropriate margins

pimpingQuestions.question: Melanocytes originate from the neural crest. How does this embryologic origin explain why melanoma can arise in non-skin locations, and name three extracutaneous sites.
pimpingQuestions.answer: Neural crest cells migrate throughout the body during development — they don't just go to skin. Melanocytes seed the uveal tract (eye), meninges, and GI mucosa during embryonic migration. This is why melanoma can arise in: (1) the eye (uveal melanoma — most common extracutaneous site), (2) mucosal surfaces (anorectal, vulvovaginal, nasopharyngeal melanoma), (3) the meninges (primary CNS melanoma). The neural crest origin also explains melanoma's propensity for neurotropism — it spreads along nerves because melanocytes are embryologically related to Schwann cells and neurons.

pimpingQuestions.question: You're excising a melanoma on the anterior shin with 2cm margins. Why is this location a nightmare for closure, and what's your reconstructive plan?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The anterior shin has minimal subcutaneous tissue, the skin is thin and inelastic, and the tibia lies immediately beneath — there's no muscle or fat to close over. The pretibial skin has poor blood supply (watershed zone between anterior tibial and posterior tibial perforators), so skin flaps are prone to necrosis. Options: (1) skin graft (split-thickness) directly onto the periosteum if intact, (2) local rotational flap from the calf (needs intact perforators), (3) free flap if the defect is large or periosteum is excised. NEVER attempt primary closure under tension on the shin — it will dehisce. Also, the saphenous nerve and great saphenous vein run medially and should be preserved if not involved.

pimpingQuestions.question: The wide local excision margins are based on Breslow depth. What are the current NCCN-recommended margins, and why don't we just take 5cm on everyone?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Current NCCN margins: melanoma in situ → 0.5-1cm, ≤1mm Breslow → 1cm, 1.01-2mm → 1-2cm, >2mm → 2cm. We don't take 5cm because: (1) randomized trials (Veronesi, Balch, Thomas) showed NO survival benefit for margins >2cm even in thick melanomas, (2) wider margins increase morbidity (larger defects, more complex closures, higher wound complications, more skin grafts), (3) melanoma spreads hematogenously/lymphatically, not by radial local extension beyond 2cm. The margin is about clearing local satellite microsatellites in the dermal lymphatics, which rarely extend beyond 2cm from the primary.

pimpingQuestions.question: Your pathology comes back and the deep margin is positive. The melanoma was 3mm Breslow depth on the upper back. What do you do?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Return to the OR for re-excision of the deep margin. On the back, the deep margin should extend to the muscular fascia — if positive, you need to take the underlying fascia (trapezius or latissimus dorsi fascia). However, routine inclusion of fascia in WLE is NO LONGER recommended by NCCN (older teaching was 'take the fascia') because trials showed no survival benefit. A positive deep margin is different from a positive lateral margin — it suggests a deeper invasive component that changes staging. Re-excise to clear fascia, update staging, and consider adjuvant therapy (immunotherapy with pembrolizumab if stage IIB or higher).

pimpingQuestions.question: Your patient has a 0.9mm melanoma on the face. The 1cm margin would be disfiguring. What's the evidence-based alternative, and when do you fight for the full margin?
pimpingQuestions.answer: For facial melanoma, Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) or staged margin-controlled excision ('slow Mohs') achieves complete margin clearance with tissue conservation. MMS examines 100% of the peripheral and deep margins (vs standard breadloaf pathology which samples <1%). For cosmetically sensitive areas (face, ears, hands, feet), NCCN allows narrower clinical margins when combined with complete histologic margin assessment. Fight for the full margin when: (1) the melanoma is >1mm Breslow, (2) it's ulcerated, (3) it has neurotropism, or (4) it's a desmoplastic subtype — these have higher local recurrence rates that justify wider excision even with cosmetic cost. Function and oncologic clearance trump cosmesis.

name: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (Melanoma)
overview: Minimally invasive lymph node staging for melanoma
positioning: Depends on primary site and node basin

pimpingQuestions.question: Lymph nodes develop from mesenchymal condensations along lymphatic vessels during the 5th month of gestation. Why does melanoma preferentially metastasize to lymph nodes before other organs, and how does neural crest biology play into this?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Melanoma cells retain neural crest migratory properties — they have high motility and express adhesion molecules (like MCAM/MUC18) that facilitate lymphatic invasion. Lymphatic vessels are the first drainage pathway encountered in the dermis, and melanoma cells are biologically 'programmed' for migration (their embryologic ancestors migrated from the neural tube to the skin). Additionally, the lymph node microenvironment provides growth factors and immune-privileged niches (subcapsular sinus macrophages) that support melanoma cell survival. This is why nodal metastasis often precedes hematogenous spread — it's the path of least resistance for migratory cells.

pimpingQuestions.question: You inject technetium and blue dye around a melanoma on the posterior shoulder. Lymphoscintigraphy shows drainage to BOTH axillary AND posterior triangle cervical nodes. How common is this ambiguous drainage, and what do you do?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Ambiguous or interval/in-transit drainage to multiple basins occurs in 5-15% of truncal melanomas, especially those near the midline or watershed zones (posterior shoulder is notorious). You MUST biopsy sentinel nodes in ALL draining basins — missing a positive node in one basin while only sampling the other is an oncologic failure. This is exactly why preoperative lymphoscintigraphy is MANDATORY, not optional. Intraoperatively, use both blue dye and gamma probe in each basin. The posterior shoulder is the classic 'trick question' location because of its complex watershed drainage between axillary, cervical, and sometimes supraclavicular basins.

pimpingQuestions.question: During axillary SLNB for melanoma, you encounter a blue-stained node immediately adjacent to the axillary vein. What named vascular structure are you most worried about injuring, and what happens if you do?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The thoracodorsal vascular pedicle (thoracodorsal artery and vein) — branch of the subscapular artery from the axillary artery. It runs along the lateral border of the latissimus dorsi and passes through the central axilla near the axillary vein. If you ligate it, the latissimus dorsi muscle loses its dominant blood supply, which matters enormously if the patient later needs a latissimus dorsi flap for breast reconstruction (not relevant in melanoma) or if you need it as a local flap. Also protect the lateral thoracic artery — it runs on the chest wall surface and can bleed briskly if transected.

pimpingQuestions.question: SLNB is 'minimally invasive' but has a 5-10% complication rate. What is the most common complication of axillary SLNB, and how does it differ from the most common complication after completion lymph node dissection?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Most common after SLNB: seroma (5-8%) — a contained lymphatic fluid collection at the biopsy site. It's self-limited and usually resolves with 1-2 aspirations. Most common after completion lymph node dissection: lymphedema (15-25%) — permanent arm swelling from disruption of the entire lymphatic drainage pathway. This is the critical difference and the entire RATIONALE for SLNB: by only removing 1-3 sentinel nodes (instead of all 20-30 axillary nodes), you preserve lymphatic drainage and reduce lymphedema risk from 25% to <5%. Other SLNB complications: wound infection (3-5%), nerve injury (numbness from intercostobrachial nerve, which you can't always preserve even in SLNB).

pimpingQuestions.question: MSLT-II showed that completion lymph node dissection doesn't improve survival in sentinel node-positive melanoma. So why do we still do SLNB at all?
pimpingQuestions.answer: SLNB provides the single most powerful prognostic information in melanoma — sentinel node status changes the staging, the prognosis, AND the adjuvant therapy decision. A positive SLNB upstages a 2mm melanoma from stage IIA (80% 10-year survival) to stage IIIA (78%) or IIIB (60%) depending on tumor burden. This triggers eligibility for adjuvant immunotherapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) which has been shown in KEYNOTE-716 and CheckMate-238 to improve recurrence-free survival. So while we may not do completion dissection anymore (MSLT-II), the SLNB result is the gatekeeper to life-extending adjuvant treatment. It's prognostic and therapeutic decision-making, not just staging theater.

name: Rotational Flap Closure
overview: Reconstruction using rotational skin flap for wound coverage

pimpingQuestions.question: Skin develops from two embryologic layers — what are they, and why does understanding this matter when designing a rotational flap?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Epidermis from surface ectoderm, dermis from mesoderm (specifically dermatome portion of somitic mesoderm for trunk, neural crest-derived mesenchyme for face). This matters because the dermal blood supply — the subdermal plexus — lives at the ectodermal-mesodermal interface. A rotational flap must include the FULL-THICKNESS dermis to capture this plexus. If you elevate too superficially (splitting the dermis), you devascularize the flap. For face flaps, the neural crest-derived dermis is thinner and more vascular than trunk dermis, which is why facial flaps survive on narrower pedicles.

pimpingQuestions.question: You're designing a rotation flap on the scalp. What unique anatomic feature of the scalp makes it both ideal and challenging for rotational flaps?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The galea aponeurotica — a tough fascial layer connecting frontalis to occipitalis muscles. Ideal because: the scalp's blood supply runs in the subgaleal plane (superficial temporal, supraorbital, supratrochlear, occipital, posterior auricular arteries), creating one of the most robust vascular networks in the body — scalp flaps rarely die. Challenging because: the galea is inelastic, severely limiting tissue advancement. To gain mobility, you must SCORE the galea with parallel incisions (galeal scoring) — each score gives 1-1.5cm of additional advancement. Without scoring, the scalp will not rotate. Back-cut the flap if needed, but never violate the subgaleal vascular plexus.

pimpingQuestions.question: What's the difference between a random-pattern and an axial-pattern rotational flap, and when does the distinction matter clinically?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Random-pattern flap: relies on the subdermal plexus without a named vessel. Limited to a length-to-width ratio of ~2:1 on the body (3:1 on the face due to richer vascularity). Axial-pattern flap: designed around a specific named artery (e.g., superficial temporal artery for a temporoparietal flap). Can be much longer because the named vessel provides guaranteed perfusion along the flap's axis. The distinction matters when: (1) the defect is large — random flaps can't safely reach, (2) the tissue has been irradiated — random perfusion is unreliable in irradiated tissue, axial vessels may still be patent, (3) you need to import tissue from a distance (axial flaps can be tubed and transferred in stages).

pimpingQuestions.question: Post-op day 2, the distal tip of your rotation flap is dusky and cool. What's happening and what's your decision framework?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Distal flap ischemia — the farthest point from the pedicle has insufficient perfusion. Decision framework: (1) Is it venous congestion (purple, swollen, brisk dark bleeding on pinprick) or arterial insufficiency (pale, cool, no capillary refill)? Venous: release sutures at the tip to decompress, consider leeches (medicinal Hirudo) for salvage. Arterial: the flap was designed too long for its pedicle width. (2) Observe for 48-72 hours — partial thickness necrosis (eschar) often demarcates and heals by secondary intention. (3) If full-thickness necrosis: debride and plan secondary closure, skin graft, or a second flap. The key lesson: when designing the flap, the arc of rotation should be generous — always make the flap BIGGER than you think you need. You can trim excess; you can't add tissue.

pimpingQuestions.question: You're closing a defect after melanoma excision on the cheek. The plastic surgery intern suggests a rotation flap. When should you use a rotation flap versus a skin graft versus healing by secondary intention?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Rotation flap: best when you need COLOR MATCH, TEXTURE MATCH, and CONTOUR (cheek is ideal — adjacent tissue provides all three). Use when: defect is <5cm, adjacent tissue is lax enough, and there's no concern for recurrence requiring re-excision. Skin graft: use when the defect is too large for local flaps, the wound bed is well-vascularized (not over bone/tendon), or you need to MONITOR for local recurrence (grafts are thin and transparent — you can see recurrence through them; flaps hide it). Secondary intention: surprisingly good on concave surfaces (medial canthus, temple, nasolabial fold) — healing by contraction produces excellent cosmesis in these areas. The attending pearl: on the face, NEVER skin graft if a local flap is possible — the cosmetic difference is dramatic and patients live with their face every day.

name: Negative Pressure Wound Therapy (Wound VAC)
overview: Application of controlled negative pressure for wound healing

pimpingQuestions.question: Wound healing recapitulates embryonic tissue development — specifically the three phases of wound repair mirror embryologic tissue formation. How does negative pressure wound therapy accelerate this recapitulation?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The three phases: inflammation (like mesenchymal condensation), proliferation (like organogenesis), remodeling (like tissue maturation). NPWT accelerates the transition from inflammation to proliferation by: (1) removing inhibitory inflammatory exudate (cytokines like MMPs that destroy new matrix), (2) applying mechanical strain (microdeformation) to wound bed cells — this mechanotransduction stimulates cell proliferation and angiogenesis, mimicking the mechanical forces that drive embryonic tissue morphogenesis, (3) reducing bacterial bioburden. The suction literally 'pulls' granulation tissue formation forward, similar to how mechanical forces guide embryonic cell migration and differentiation.

pimpingQuestions.question: You're placing a wound VAC on an open abdomen after damage control laparotomy. What must you place between the viscera and the sponge, and what abdominal structure is most vulnerable to injury?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Place a non-adherent barrier layer (perforated polyethylene sheet or a commercially available visceral protective layer like the ABThera system) between the sponge and the bowel. Without this layer, the sponge adheres to serosa and creates enterocutaneous fistulae when changed. The most vulnerable structure is the duodenum (specifically the ligament of Treitz area) and any fresh anastomosis — suction on an anastomosis will cause a leak. Also, the greater omentum, if present, should be draped over the bowel as a biological protective layer before placing the barrier sheet.

pimpingQuestions.question: NPWT works by promoting angiogenesis and granulation. What's the optimal negative pressure setting, and what happens to wound bed blood flow at pressures that are too high?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Optimal pressure: -125 mmHg (the Morykwas study). At this pressure, blood flow to the wound bed increases by 4x. At pressures above -200 mmHg, blood flow paradoxically DECREASES — the suction collapses capillaries and creates ischemia in the wound bed, which is counterproductive. Below -75 mmHg, the mechanical deformation is insufficient to stimulate cell proliferation. The goldilocks zone is -75 to -125 mmHg. Use lower pressures (-75) on skin grafts to prevent shear displacement of the graft, and -125 for open wounds requiring granulation. Continuous mode for the first 48 hours, then intermittent (5 min on/2 min off) may further enhance granulation.

pimpingQuestions.question: The nurse calls at 2 AM — the wound VAC alarm is going off constantly, showing 'leak.' The patient has a large abdominal wound VAC. Before you drive in, what do you troubleshoot by phone?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Phone troubleshoot checklist: (1) Check all drape edges — the most common cause of leak is loss of seal at the adhesive drape edge, especially near skin folds, scars, or moist skin. Fix with skin prep (benzoin) and additional drape strips. (2) Check tubing connections — ensure the pad connector (TRAC pad) is firmly seated and the tubing isn't kinked. (3) Check canister — full canister triggers an alarm. Replace it. (4) Have the nurse press the drape firmly over the sponge while the suction cycles — if the alarm stops, the leak is at the drape-skin interface. (5) If the wound is producing more than 500mL/day, consider whether the VAC is appropriate — high-output wounds may need continuous wall suction until output decreases. Most VAC alarms are drape seal failures and don't require surgeon presence.

pimpingQuestions.question: Name three wounds where NPWT is absolutely CONTRAINDICATED, and explain why each would be dangerous.
pimpingQuestions.answer: (1) Exposed blood vessels (especially anastomotic sites or grafts) — suction can erode the vessel wall and cause catastrophic hemorrhage. There are case reports of fatal exsanguination from VAC-induced aortic erosion. (2) Untreated osteomyelitis — NPWT will granulate OVER infected bone without treating the underlying infection, creating a false sense of healing while sepsis brews beneath. Debride the infected bone first. (3) Malignant wounds (fungating tumors) — NPWT stimulates cell proliferation via mechanotransduction, and you do NOT want to stimulate proliferation of cancer cells. It can accelerate tumor growth. Also relative contraindication: patients on anticoagulation with friable wound beds — the increased blood flow can cause significant hemorrhage into the canister.

name: Lipoma Excision
overview: Surgical removal of benign fatty tumors

pimpingQuestions.question: Lipomas arise from which embryologic tissue, and what genetic alteration is most commonly associated?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Mesodermal adipose tissue. The most common genetic alteration is rearrangement of the HMGA2 gene on chromosome 12q14-15 (found in ~65% of solitary lipomas). Lipomas are the most common soft tissue tumor. Malignant transformation to liposarcoma is extremely rare in superficial lipomas.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the surgical plane for lipoma excision?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Lipomas have a thin fibrous capsule that separates them from surrounding tissue. Dissection in the plane between the capsule and surrounding fat allows en bloc removal. Staying on the capsule prevents bleeding from surrounding tissue perforators. Squeezing the lipoma through a small incision (enucleation technique) works for small (<5cm) superficial lipomas.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply to a lipoma, and why do large/deep lipomas bleed more?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Lipomas receive blood supply from perforating vessels that enter the capsule. Small superficial lipomas have minimal vascularity. Large or deep (subfascial) lipomas develop more robust blood supply from muscular perforators and can bleed significantly. Intramuscular lipomas (within the muscle belly) are particularly vascular and may require deeper dissection with hemostasis.

pimpingQuestions.question: When should you be concerned that a 'lipoma' is actually a liposarcoma?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Red flags: >5cm, deep to fascia (subfascial/intramuscular), rapidly growing, painful, heterogeneous on imaging (non-uniform fat signal on MRI). Any deep soft tissue mass >5cm should have MRI before excision and may need core needle biopsy. Well-differentiated liposarcoma (atypical lipomatous tumor) can be indistinguishable from lipoma on physical exam.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the recurrence rate after lipoma excision?
pimpingQuestions.answer: 1-3% for complete excision (including capsule). Higher for: intramuscular lipomas (incomplete excision due to infiltrative borders — 20%), multiple lipomatosis (familial, Dercum's disease, Madelung disease), and angiolipomas. Recurrence usually represents incomplete capsule removal rather than true recurrence.

name: Sebaceous Cyst Excision
overview: Surgical removal of epidermoid or sebaceous cysts

pimpingQuestions.question: Most 'sebaceous cysts' are actually epidermal inclusion cysts. What's the embryologic difference between a true sebaceous cyst, an epidermoid cyst, and a dermoid cyst?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Epidermoid cyst (most common): lined by keratinized stratified squamous epithelium (ectodermal origin) — contains laminated keratin ('cheesy' material). Arises from implantation of surface ectoderm into the dermis (trauma, surgery) or occlusion of a pilosebaceous unit. TRUE sebaceous cyst (steatocystoma): lined by sebaceous gland epithelium (also ectodermal), contains oily/sebaceous material, NOT keratin. Rare. Dermoid cyst: contains BOTH ectodermal AND mesodermal elements (hair follicles, sweat glands, even cartilage) — arises from embryonic inclusion of ectoderm along fusion lines during development. Found at embryonic fusion sites: lateral brow, midline nose, midline scalp. Dermoids can have intracranial extension — always image before excising a midline nasal dermoid.

pimpingQuestions.question: You're excising a 'sebaceous cyst' from the posterior triangle of the neck. What's in the posterior triangle that can ruin your day during a 'simple' cyst excision?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) — it crosses the posterior triangle superficially, running from the posterior border of the SCM to the anterior border of the trapezius. It's the most commonly injured nerve in elective neck surgery. Injury causes trapezius denervation: shoulder drop, inability to abduct the arm above 90°, chronic shoulder pain, and scapular winging. The nerve is often embedded in or immediately deep to the fascia that these cysts sit on. Other structures at risk: external jugular vein (crosses superficially), transverse cervical artery, and the brachial plexus trunks deep in the triangle.

pimpingQuestions.question: The cyst ruptured during your dissection and now the wound is contaminated with keratin debris. What happens if you leave keratin fragments behind, and how does this relate to the blood supply of the wound?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Keratin is a potent foreign body inflammatory stimulus — retained keratin fragments incite a vigorous granulomatous inflammatory reaction, creating painful nodules, chronic drainage, and recurrence. The tissue becomes highly vascularized (inflammation = neovascularization), forming granulation tissue around each keratin fragment. This is actually a foreign body giant cell reaction — macrophages fuse into Langhans-type giant cells trying to engulf the keratin. Prevention: irrigate copiously after rupture, meticulously remove all visible cyst wall fragments and keratin material. If the cyst was infected at the time of excision, the recurrence rate jumps to 30% (vs 3% for intact excision) because of retained microscopic wall fragments.

pimpingQuestions.question: Your pathology comes back and the 'sebaceous cyst' shows squamous cell carcinoma arising within the cyst wall. How common is this, and what do you do now?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Malignant transformation of an epidermal inclusion cyst to SCC is rare (<1%) but well-documented, especially in long-standing cysts. Risk factors: chronic inflammation, prior radiation to the area, immunosuppression. The SCC arising in a cyst tends to be well-differentiated but can be locally aggressive. Management: (1) review margins — if the cyst was excised intact with clear margins, this may be adequate for well-differentiated SCC, (2) if margins are positive or close, re-excise with appropriate margins (typically 1cm for well-differentiated SCC), (3) consider nodal evaluation if the SCC is high-risk (>2cm, poorly differentiated, perineural invasion), (4) refer to surgical oncology for formal staging. The lesson: ALWAYS send 'simple' cyst specimens to pathology. It's not always what you think.

pimpingQuestions.question: The patient has an infected, erythematous, fluctuant 'sebaceous cyst.' The ED drained it and packed it. Now it's back, bigger than before. What's the correct management algorithm?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The correct algorithm: (1) ACUTE phase (infected/inflamed): I&D and packing only — do NOT attempt excision in an infected field. The cyst wall is friable, planes are obliterated, and you'll leave fragments behind (guaranteed recurrence). (2) WAIT 4-6 weeks for inflammation to completely resolve — the cyst wall will reconstitute and become a defined, dissectable structure again. (3) DEFINITIVE excision once cool: make an elliptical incision INCORPORATING the punctum (the central pore), dissect the cyst in toto without rupturing it. The punctum is the key — it's the communication between the cyst and the surface, and leaving it guarantees recurrence. The rookie mistake: trying to be a hero by excising during the acute infection. Patience here prevents recurrence and gives a better cosmetic result.

name: Total Pelvic Exenteration
overview: Complete removal of pelvic organs for advanced malignancy

pimpingQuestions.question: Total pelvic exenteration removes all organs from the endodermal cloaca derivatives and the mesodermal urogenital structures. What embryologic structure gave rise to ALL the pelvic organs you're removing, and what does the cloacal membrane separate?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The cloaca — the common endodermal chamber that receives the hindgut, allantois, and mesonephric ducts. The urorectal septum divides it into the urogenital sinus (anterior → bladder, urethra, prostate/vagina) and the anorectal canal (posterior → rectum, upper anal canal). The cloacal membrane separates endoderm from surface ectoderm. In total pelvic exenteration, you're removing everything the cloaca became: rectum (hindgut), bladder (urogenital sinus), internal reproductive organs (mesonephric/paramesonephric derivatives), and the pelvic peritoneum. You're essentially undoing embryologic pelvic organ formation.

pimpingQuestions.question: The lateral dissection plane during pelvic exenteration follows the pelvic sidewall. What structures define the lateral limit of resection, and what nerve plexus lives there?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The lateral limit is the internal iliac vessels and the obturator internus muscle/fascia along the pelvic sidewall. The internal iliac artery branches (superior and inferior vesical, middle rectal, obturator, internal pudendal) are individually ligated and divided. The obturator nerve and vessels run through the obturator canal and must be identified and preserved if not involved by tumor. The inferior hypogastric (pelvic) nerve plexus is on the lateral pelvic sidewall — it's almost always sacrificed in total exenteration, resulting in complete loss of bladder, sexual, and rectal function (which is moot since all three organs are removed). The ureters cross the pelvic brim and must be identified early.

pimpingQuestions.question: After removing all pelvic organs, you have a massive dead space in the pelvis. How do you fill it, and what's the blood supply of the flap you use?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Fill the pelvic dead space with a vertical rectus abdominis myocutaneous (VRAM) flap — the workhorse for pelvic exenteration reconstruction. Blood supply: the deep inferior epigastric artery (from the external iliac), which runs on the posterior surface of the rectus muscle. The flap is harvested with the rectus muscle, its overlying skin paddle, and the DIEA pedicle, then rotated into the pelvis to obliterate dead space. Benefits: brings well-vascularized tissue into an irradiated field, prevents small bowel from falling into the pelvis and adhering/fistulizing, and can reconstruct the vagina if needed. Without a flap, pelvic abscess and small bowel fistula rates are unacceptably high.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the overall 30-day morbidity rate for total pelvic exenteration, and what is the single most common major complication?
pimpingQuestions.answer: 30-day morbidity: 50-70% — this is one of the highest-morbidity elective operations in surgery. The single most common major complication: wound/pelvic infection and abscess (25-40%). The pelvic dead space, combined with the fact that most patients have received prior chemoradiation (irradiated tissue has poor vascularity and impaired immune response), creates an ideal environment for infection. Other major complications: ureteroileal anastomotic leak (urinary conduit leak, 10-15%), small bowel obstruction (15-20%), and VTE (10-15%). Operative mortality has improved to 2-5% in experienced centers but was historically 10-20%. This operation is ONLY justified when it offers the only chance of cure — there is no palliative exenteration.

pimpingQuestions.question: A 45-year-old woman with recurrent cervical cancer after chemoradiation is being evaluated for total pelvic exenteration. What's the ONE preoperative finding on exam or imaging that makes you cancel the operation?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Tumor fixed to the pelvic sidewall — specifically encasement of the internal iliac vessels or sciatic nerve involvement. If the tumor is fixed laterally, you cannot achieve an R0 resection without removing the pelvic sidewall musculature and vessels, which is incompatible with limb viability. This finding on exam (the 'frozen pelvis') or imaging (encasement of internal iliac vessels, sciatic/obturator nerve involvement, or extension through the greater sciatic foramen) converts the case from potentially curative to unresectable. Other findings that should make you pause: bilateral ureteral obstruction with renal failure (suggests extensive bilateral sidewall disease), distant metastases (liver, lung, para-aortic nodes above the aortic bifurcation), and medical fitness — this operation requires 8-12 hours of anesthesia in a patient who's already been through chemoradiation.

name: Cytoreductive Surgery with HIPEC
overview: Maximal tumor debulking followed by heated intraperitoneal chemotherapy
positioning: Supine with access to entire abdomen

pimpingQuestions.question: The peritoneum develops from lateral plate mesoderm. Peritoneal carcinomatosis from colorectal cancer follows a specific embryologic-based distribution pattern. Why does the cancer preferentially deposit in certain peritoneal regions?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Peritoneal metastases follow gravity, fluid circulation patterns, and embryologic peritoneal reflections. Preferential sites: (1) the pouch of Douglas/rectovesical pouch (most dependent area in the pelvis — gravity), (2) the right paracolic gutter (peritoneal fluid circulates clockwise from pelvis up the right gutter to the right subdiaphragmatic space), (3) the greater omentum (the 'policeman of the abdomen' — rich in milky spots containing macrophages that TRAP tumor cells), (4) the ligament of Treitz (a peritoneal fold that creates a stasis point). The Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) maps these 13 regions to quantify disease burden — understanding the embryologic basis of peritoneal reflections is essential for complete cytoreduction.

pimpingQuestions.question: During cytoreduction, you need to perform a total parietal peritonectomy of the right upper quadrant. What structures are you stripping peritoneum off of, and what structure absolutely CANNOT be injured during this dissection?
pimpingQuestions.answer: You're stripping peritoneum off: the right hemidiaphragm, the liver surface (Glisson's capsule), the right subhepatic space (Morrison's pouch), the right paracolic gutter, and the hepatoduodenal ligament. The structure that CANNOT be injured: the right hepatic vein (and the IVC behind the liver). The peritoneum on the bare area of the liver (where the liver directly contacts the diaphragm without peritoneal covering) is the most treacherous zone — here the hepatic veins are just beneath the surface, and a tear means potentially fatal hemorrhage. Also, the right phrenic nerve on the diaphragm — injury causes right hemidiaphragm paralysis and respiratory compromise.

pimpingQuestions.question: HIPEC uses heated chemotherapy (typically mitomycin C or oxaliplatin) circulated in the abdomen at 41-43°C. Why does hyperthermia enhance drug cytotoxicity, and at what temperature do you start causing thermal injury to normal tissue?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Hyperthermia enhances cytotoxicity through: (1) increased drug penetration into tumor nodules (heat increases cell membrane permeability), (2) impaired DNA repair mechanisms in cancer cells at elevated temperatures, (3) synergistic cytotoxicity — heat makes cancer cells 10-100x more sensitive to alkylating agents, (4) increased tumor blood flow and drug delivery via vasodilation. Thermal injury to normal tissue begins at 43°C — the therapeutic window is narrow (41-43°C). Above 43°C, protein denaturation begins in normal peritoneal mesothelial cells, increasing the risk of anastomotic leaks, bowel injury, and adhesion formation. Intraperitoneal temperature is continuously monitored at 4+ points to ensure homogeneous heating without exceeding 43°C.

pimpingQuestions.question: The single most feared surgical complication after CRS/HIPEC is anastomotic leak. Why is the leak rate higher than standard colorectal surgery, and what's the reported rate?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Leak rates after CRS/HIPEC: 5-12% (vs 3-6% for standard colorectal). Higher because: (1) chemotherapy (especially mitomycin C) directly impairs wound healing by inhibiting fibroblast proliferation at the anastomotic level, (2) hyperthermia causes thermal injury to the serosal surface of the bowel, weakening the wall, (3) extensive peritonectomy devascularizes bowel mesentery in areas adjacent to the stripping, (4) these are long operations (8-12 hours) with significant third-spacing, hypoalbuminemia, and physiologic stress. Some surgeons delay the HIPEC until AFTER performing all anastomoses to protect the suture lines. Others use a protective diverting ileostomy for all rectal anastomoses. If in doubt, bring out a stoma — a leak in this setting has 30-50% mortality.

pimpingQuestions.question: A patient with colorectal peritoneal carcinomatosis has a PCI of 22. You open the abdomen and confirm extensive disease. Do you proceed with CRS/HIPEC? What's the magic number?
pimpingQuestions.answer: NO — close and abort. The magic number for colorectal carcinomatosis is PCI ≤20 (some centers use ≤17). Above PCI 20, complete cytoreduction (CC-0/CC-1) is rarely achievable, and the survival benefit disappears — you're inflicting massive morbidity (50-70% complication rate) without oncologic benefit. The Sugarbaker data clearly shows: CC-0 resection + HIPEC with PCI ≤20 = median survival 30-50 months. PCI >20 or CC-2/3 = median survival 6-12 months (same as systemic chemo alone, but with 10x the morbidity). The other absolute contraindications to proceeding: unresectable hepatic metastases, retroperitoneal invasion, massive small bowel involvement requiring resection of >1.5m of small bowel (short gut), and poor performance status. The hardest part of this operation is knowing when NOT to do it.

name: Pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt)
overview: Division of pyloric muscle for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the embryologic basis of pyloric stenosis?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Idiopathic hypertrophy of the circular smooth muscle of the pylorus (derived from foregut mesoderm). Not present at birth -- develops over the first 2-6 weeks. Incidence: 1-3/1000 live births, 4:1 male predominance (firstborn males). Multifactorial inheritance with environmental factors (erythromycin exposure in first 2 weeks).

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the 'olive' sign and what imaging confirms pyloric stenosis?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The 'olive' is a palpable, firm mass in the right upper quadrant/epigastrium -- the hypertrophied pylorus. Ultrasound confirms: pyloric muscle thickness >3mm, pyloric channel length >15mm, and pyloric diameter >14mm. Sensitivity: 95-100%.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the Ramstedt pyloromyotomy technique?
pimpingQuestions.answer: A longitudinal incision through the serosa and hypertrophied circular muscle of the pylorus, down to but NOT through the submucosa/mucosa. The muscle fibers are spread (not cut) to allow the submucosa to bulge through. The myotomy extends from the pyloric vein of Mayo proximally to the antral transition zone.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply at the pylorus relevant to pyloromyotomy?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Right gastric artery (along lesser curve) and right gastroepiploic artery (along greater curve). The pyloric vein of Mayo marks the distal extent of the pyloromyotomy on the duodenal side -- extending past this landmark risks duodenal perforation. The GDA runs posterior to D1.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the most feared complication of pyloromyotomy?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Unrecognized mucosal perforation (1-4%) -- usually at the duodenal end of the myotomy. Detected by: air insufflation via orogastric tube with saline over the myotomy site (bubbles = perforation). If found: primary repair with omental patch, rotate the pyloromyotomy 180 degrees and perform a second myotomy on the opposite side. Missed perforation causes peritonitis.

name: Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia Repair
overview: Surgical repair of congenital diaphragmatic defect

pimpingQuestions.question: CDH results from failure of which embryologic structure to close?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The pleuroperitoneal membrane (posterolateral component of the diaphragm). Left-sided (Bochdalek hernia): 80-85%. Right-sided: 10-15%. Bilateral: 5%. The defect allows abdominal viscera to herniate into the thorax, compressing the ipsilateral lung and causing pulmonary hypoplasia. Incidence: 1:2500 births.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the pathophysiology of CDH beyond the diaphragmatic defect?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Pulmonary hypoplasia (both lungs, worse ipsilateral): reduced alveolar number, reduced pulmonary vascular cross-section, and abnormal surfactant. Persistent pulmonary hypertension (PPHN): muscularized pulmonary arterioles cause right-to-left shunting through the PDA and foramen ovale. The respiratory failure is the lethal component, not the hernia itself.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply to the hypoplastic lung?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Pulmonary arteries are present but have increased medial muscular hypertrophy causing high PVR. Bronchial arteries supply the airway. The hypoplastic lung has reduced total vascular bed -- this is why pulmonary hypertension is the primary cause of mortality (50% of severe CDH).

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the current management strategy -- when do you operate?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Stabilize first, operate later. Initial management: gentle ventilation (avoid barotrauma -- high PEEP/pressures worsen PPHN), iNO (pulmonary vasodilator), ECMO (for refractory hypoxemia). Surgery is performed AFTER physiologic stabilization (usually 24-72 hours) when pulmonary hypertension improves. Emergent surgery in the delivery room has been abandoned.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the surgical repair technique?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Subcostal (abdominal) approach: reduce the herniated viscera, assess the defect. Small defects: primary closure with interrupted non-absorbable sutures. Large defects (>50% of hemidiaphragm or agenesis): prosthetic patch (Gore-Tex/PTFE). Thoracoscopic repair is gaining popularity for smaller defects in stable patients. Mortality: 10-30% overall (varies with severity, worse with liver-up and low lung-to-head ratio on prenatal US).

name: Hirschsprung Pull-Through
overview: Surgical treatment for Hirschsprung disease with aganglionic bowel

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the embryologic basis of Hirschsprung disease?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Failure of vagal neural crest cell migration to the distal bowel during weeks 5-12. The ganglion cells (Meissner's/Auerbach's plexus) are absent in the affected segment. Migration proceeds cranial to caudal -- the rectosigmoid is most commonly affected (80%). Total colonic aganglionosis: 5-8%.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the transition zone anatomy?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The junction between ganglionic (normal, proximal, dilated) and aganglionic (abnormal, distal, narrow/spastic) bowel. This is identified by serial seromuscular biopsies with frozen section during surgery. The pull-through brings ganglionic bowel to the anal canal. The transition zone is usually in the rectosigmoid.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply that supports the pull-through?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The pulled-through bowel depends on its mesenteric blood supply (IMA branches for sigmoid, SMA for more proximal bowel). The mesentery must reach the perineum without tension or twist. For long-segment disease, extensive mesenteric lengthening may be required.

pimpingQuestions.question: What are the three main pull-through techniques?
pimpingQuestions.answer: 1) Swenson: full-thickness excision of aganglionic rectum, primary anastomosis at the anal canal. 2) Duhamel: aganglionic rectum left in situ posteriorly, ganglionic bowel brought behind it (side-to-side anastomosis). 3) Soave (endorectal): aganglionic mucosa stripped, ganglionic bowel pulled through the aganglionic muscular cuff. Soave is most commonly performed today, increasingly as a single-stage transanal approach.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC)?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The most serious complication (10-30%), can occur before or after surgery. Presents with explosive diarrhea, abdominal distension, fever, and sepsis. Caused by bacterial overgrowth and mucosal inflammation in the dilated proximal bowel. Can be fatal if not treated with rectal irrigation, IV antibiotics, and decompression. It is the leading cause of death in Hirschsprung disease.

name: Undescended Testis (Orchiopexy)
overview: Surgical repositioning of undescended testis into scrotum

pimpingQuestions.question: What guides testicular descent embryologically?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The gubernaculum testis (mesenchymal cord from inferior testis to labioscrotal swelling). Two phases: transabdominal (weeks 10-15, INSL3/insulin-like factor 3-dependent) and inguinoscrotal (weeks 25-35, testosterone/CGRP-dependent through the processus vaginalis). Arrest at any point: cryptorchidism.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the most common location of an undescended testis?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Inguinal canal (72%) -- between the internal and external rings. Other: abdominal (8%), high scrotal (20%), ectopic (perineal, femoral, contralateral -- rare). Nonpalpable testis (20% of cryptorchidism) requires laparoscopy to determine if the testis is intra-abdominal, absent (vanished/atrophied), or peeping (at the internal ring).

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply to the undescended testis during orchiopexy?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Three arteries: testicular (from aorta -- primary), deferential (from inferior vesical), cremasteric (from inferior epigastric). For a high abdominal testis, the testicular artery may be too short -- Fowler-Stephens technique: divide the testicular artery (relying on deferential and cremasteric collaterals) to gain length for scrotal placement. Two-stage Fowler-Stephens improves collateral development.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the optimal age for orchiopexy?
pimpingQuestions.answer: 6-12 months of age (AUA/AAP guidelines). Benefits of early surgery: preserves fertility potential (germ cells degenerate with prolonged cryptorchidism -- 40% reduction in fertility per year of undescent after age 1), reduces malignancy risk (6-10x increased cancer risk vs normal), and better psychological outcomes. After age 2, germ cell loss is significant and irreversible.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the malignancy risk in an undescended testis?
pimpingQuestions.answer: 4-10x increased risk of testicular cancer (seminoma most common). Orchiopexy before age 10 reduces but does not eliminate the cancer risk. Orchiopexy after puberty does not reduce cancer risk -- but it does place the testis in a palpable/examinable position. An intra-abdominal testis that cannot be brought down in a post-pubertal male should be removed (orchiectomy) due to cancer risk.

name: Paracentesis
overview: Percutaneous drainage of peritoneal fluid

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the embryologic origin of the peritoneal cavity?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The intraembryonic coelom (from lateral plate mesoderm). The peritoneum (visceral and parietal) is mesothelium derived from somatic mesoderm (parietal) and splanchnic mesoderm (visceral). Ascitic fluid accumulates in the peritoneal cavity between these two layers.

pimpingQuestions.question: Where is the preferred site for paracentesis, and what is the anatomic rationale?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Left lower quadrant, two finger-breadths medial and two finger-breadths cephalad to the ASIS (lateral to the rectus sheath, in the avascular zone between the rectus and oblique muscles). This avoids the inferior epigastric artery (runs within the rectus sheath), the cecum (right-sided), and any organomegaly.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply at risk during paracentesis?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Inferior epigastric artery (runs within the posterior rectus sheath from external iliac artery). Puncturing lateral to the rectus sheath avoids this vessel. Superficial epigastric and superficial circumflex iliac arteries may be present subcutaneously. In cirrhotic patients with portal hypertension, dilated abdominal wall veins (caput medusae) increase bleeding risk.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the most common complication of large-volume paracentesis?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction (PPCD): reduction in effective arterial blood volume after removing >5L of ascites, causing renal impairment and hyponatremia. Prevention: IV albumin infusion (6-8g per liter of ascites removed above 5L). Without albumin, PPCD occurs in 70-80% of large-volume paracenteses.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the SAAG and its diagnostic significance?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Serum-Ascites Albumin Gradient: serum albumin minus ascites albumin. SAAG ≥1.1 g/dL: portal hypertension (cirrhosis, heart failure, Budd-Chiari). SAAG <1.1: non-portal hypertensive (malignancy, TB peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatitis). This is more accurate than the outdated exudate/transudate classification.

name: Thoracentesis
overview: Percutaneous drainage of pleural fluid
positioning: Sitting leaning forward or lateral decubitus

pimpingQuestions.question: The pleural cavity develops from the intraembryonic coelom. What determines pleural fluid accumulation?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Starling forces: fluid enters the pleural space from parietal pleural capillaries (systemic pressure) and is absorbed by visceral pleural capillaries (lower pressure) and lymphatics. Effusions form from: increased hydrostatic pressure (CHF), decreased oncotic pressure (hypoalbuminemia), increased capillary permeability (inflammation/infection), or impaired lymphatic drainage (malignancy).

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the anatomic landmark for thoracentesis?
pimpingQuestions.answer: 1-2 intercostal spaces below the fluid level (confirmed by percussion dullness or ultrasound), in the posterior axillary line. Insert ABOVE the rib (neurovascular bundle runs below). US-guided thoracentesis is now standard of care -- reduces pneumothorax rate from 5-10% to <1%.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the intercostal artery anatomy at the thoracentesis site?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The main intercostal artery runs in the costal groove along the inferior rib border (protected by the rib overhang). However, a collateral intercostal artery runs along the SUPERIOR border of the rib below -- the true 'safe' zone is the mid-intercostal space. In elderly patients, the arteries become more tortuous and exposed, increasing bleeding risk.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the maximum volume to remove in diagnostic versus therapeutic thoracentesis?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Diagnostic: 50-100 mL (enough for cell count, chemistry, culture, cytology). Therapeutic: up to 1500 mL (to avoid re-expansion pulmonary edema). If the patient develops chest tightness, cough, or the pleural pressure drops below -20 cmH2O (measured with manometry), stop the procedure.

pimpingQuestions.question: What are Light's criteria for distinguishing exudate from transudate?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Exudate if ANY of: 1) Pleural/serum protein ratio >0.5, 2) Pleural/serum LDH ratio >0.6, 3) Pleural LDH > 2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH. Sensitivity 98% for exudates but misclassifies 25% of transudates (especially diuretic-treated CHF). If clinical suspicion is transudate but Light's says exudate, check the serum-effusion albumin gradient (>1.2 g/dL supports transudate).

name: Lumbar Puncture
overview: Percutaneous access to cerebrospinal fluid

pimpingQuestions.question: The spinal cord develops from the neural tube. At what vertebral level does the conus medullaris terminate in adults?
pimpingQuestions.answer: L1-L2 in adults (L3 in neonates -- the cord ascends relative to the vertebral column during growth). LP is performed at L3-L4 or L4-L5 interspace (below the conus) to avoid spinal cord injury. The L4-L5 interspace is at the level of the iliac crests (Tuffier's line).

pimpingQuestions.question: What are the anatomic layers penetrated during lumbar puncture?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Skin -> subcutaneous fat -> supraspinous ligament -> interspinous ligament -> ligamentum flavum (felt as increased resistance/'pop') -> epidural space -> dura mater -> arachnoid mater -> subarachnoid space (CSF). The spinal needle traverses 4-6cm in adults.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the blood supply to the spinal cord at the LP level?
pimpingQuestions.answer: The anterior spinal artery (from vertebral arteries) and two posterior spinal arteries supply the cord. At the LP level (below the conus), only the cauda equina nerve roots are present -- they float freely in CSF and are pushed aside by the LP needle rather than being transected.

pimpingQuestions.question: What is the most common complication of lumbar puncture?
pimpingQuestions.answer: Post-dural puncture headache (PDPH): 10-30% with standard cutting needles, <5% with atraumatic (pencil-point) needles. Caused by persistent CSF leak through the dural puncture hole, leading to low CSF pressure and traction on pain-sensitive meningeal structures.
